lists.title: English A1 Grammar Path
lists.description: A1 grammar for learners coming from other languages, in Core Inventory order: pronouns, be, a/an and the, plurals, the present simple, third-person -s, do-support, the present continuous, there is/are, questions, and possessives.

points.title: Subject pronouns (I, you, he…)
points.function: Say who you are talking about
points.explanation:
I, you, he, she, it, we, they. English never drops the pronoun — every sentence needs its subject.

'I' is always capitalized, 'you' covers one person or many, and things and animals are usually 'it'.
points.culture_note: There is no formal 'you' in modern English — politeness lives in phrasing ('Could you…?'), not pronouns.

references.title: British Council–EAQUALS Core Inventory for General English

drills.translation: Introducing a woman in your family.
Talking about yourself and your classmates.
Introducing a man in your family.
Complimenting the person you are talking to.
drills.hint: the person you are talking to

drills.translation: Talking about a group of people.

points.title: To be — am / is / are
points.function: Say who or what someone is
points.explanation:
Three forms in the present: I am, he/she/it is, you/we/they are.

Contractions rule everyday English: I'm, he's, they're.

Unlike many languages, the verb can never be dropped: she IS a doctor.

references.title: British Council–EAQUALS Core Inventory for General English

drills.translation: Saying where you and your family are.
Saying where some people come from.

points.title: Articles: a / an
points.function: Mention something for the first time
points.explanation:
A before a consonant SOUND (a dog, a university), an before a vowel SOUND (an apple, an hour). It's about sound, not spelling.

Singular countable nouns almost always need one: she is A doctor — never 'she is doctor'.
points.culture_note: Learners whose languages have no articles: this is the single most common giveaway. When in doubt with a singular noun, add a/an.

references.title: British Council–EAQUALS Core Inventory for General English

points.title: The — pointing at a specific thing
points.function: Talk about something both of you can identify
points.explanation:
Use the when both speaker and listener know WHICH one: the door (of this room), the sun (there's only one), the book on the table (identified).

First mention gets a/an, second mention gets the: I saw a dog. The dog was black.

references.title: British Council–EAQUALS Core Inventory for General English

drills.translation: There is only one of it.

drills.hint: both of you can identify it

drills.sentence: {{answer}} keys in my hand are mine.
drills.hint: identified by the phrase after it
both of you can identify it

drills.translation: There is only one of it.
Asking in a café or a home.
drills.hint: both of you can identify it

points.title: Plural nouns (-s, -es, irregular)
points.function: Name more than one of something
points.explanation:
Most nouns add -s (books); nouns ending in -s, -sh, -ch, -x add -es (buses, watches); consonant + y becomes -ies (family → families).

A few are irregular and unavoidable: child → children, man → men, woman → women, foot → feet, person → people.

references.title: British Council–EAQUALS Core Inventory for General English

drills.translation: The body parts you stand on.

points.title: Present simple (I work)
points.function: Talk about habits and facts
points.explanation:
For habits, routines, and facts, the verb stays in its base form with I, you, we, they: I work in a bank, they live in London.

Time words love this tense: every day, usually, always, never.

references.title: British Council–EAQUALS Core Inventory for General English

drills.hint: what you do at your job

drills.translation: Saying where some people are at home.

drills.hint: look at a screen or show

points.title: Third-person -s (she works)
points.function: Talk about what he, she, or it does
points.explanation:
With he, she, and it, the present simple adds -s: she works, he plays.

Spelling follows the plural rules: go → goes, watch → watches, study → studies.

One tiny letter — and the most forgotten one in all of English.
points.culture_note: Native listeners barely notice a missing article, but a missing third-person -s ('she work') registers instantly. Worth drilling.

references.title: British Council–EAQUALS Core Inventory for General English

points.title: Do-support: questions and negation
points.function: Ask and deny in the present simple
points.explanation:
English borrows 'do' to build questions and negatives: Do you speak English? She does not (doesn't) work here.

With he/she/it use does — and then the main verb LOSES its -s: does she work (not 'does she works').

references.title: British Council–EAQUALS Core Inventory for General English

points.title: Present continuous (I am working)
points.function: Say what is happening right now
points.explanation:
Be + verb-ing for actions in progress: I am reading, she is cooking. Contrast with the present simple: I drink coffee every day (habit) vs I am drinking coffee (right now).

Spelling: make → making, run → running.

references.title: British Council–EAQUALS Core Inventory for General English

drills.sentence: I am {{answer}} a book right now.
drills.translation: What you are doing at this moment.

points.title: There is / there are
points.function: Say what exists or is available
points.explanation:
There is + singular (there is a book), there are + plural (there are two cafés). Questions flip: Is there a bank near here?

Negatives: there is no milk / there aren't any questions.

references.title: British Council–EAQUALS Core Inventory for General English

drills.sentence: There {{answer}} a book on the table.
There {{answer}} two cafés in this street.

drills.translation: Asking a group at the end of a talk.

points.title: Question words (what, where, when…)
points.function: Ask about things, places, people, and time
points.explanation:
What, where, when, who, why, how — question word first, then the helper: Where do you live? What is your name?

How old are you? (age uses how + old, never 'how many years have you').

references.title: British Council–EAQUALS Core Inventory for General English

points.title: Possessives (my, your, 's)
points.function: Say whose something is
points.explanation:
My, your, his, her, its, our, their — they never change for the thing owned: my book, my books. People take 's: my brother's car.

Watch its (possessive) vs it's (= it is) — natives get this wrong too.

references.title: British Council–EAQUALS Core Inventory for General English

drills.translation: Asking the person you are talking to.
It belongs to a family member.
The tail belongs to the dog.
drills.hint: belonging to a thing — no apostrophe

points.title: Past simple — irregular verbs
points.function: Say what happened, with the verbs that break the rules
points.explanation:
The most common English verbs have irregular pasts you must simply know.

go → went, see → saw, buy → bought, take → took, come → came, eat → ate.

Yesterday, last week, in 2020 — a finished-time cue calls for the past simple, never the perfect.

drills.sentence: She {{answer}} a great film last night.
We {{answer}} a new car in March.
He {{answer}} the early train this morning.

points.title: Present continuous vs present simple
points.function: Right now vs in general
points.explanation:
The continuous (am/is/are + -ing) is for NOW or around now: I'm reading. The simple is for habits and facts: I read every night.

Cues: at the moment, right now → continuous; always, every day, usually → simple.

drills.sentence: She {{answer}} to work by bike every day.
I'm {{answer}} for my keys at the moment.

points.title: Comparatives and superlatives
points.function: Compare two things — or crown the winner
points.explanation:
Short adjectives: -er / the -est (older, the oldest). Long adjectives: more / the most (more expensive, the most expensive).

Irregulars: good → better → the best; bad → worse → the worst; far → further.

drills.sentence: This bag is {{answer}} than that one.
It's the {{answer}} building in the city.
That was the {{answer}} meal I've ever had.
This exercise is {{answer}} difficult than the last.
The station is {{answer}} than I thought.

points.title: Going to and will
points.function: Two futures: plans vs promises and predictions
points.explanation:
be going to = a plan or something you can already see coming: I'm going to study tonight. Look — it's going to rain.

will = a decision made now, a promise, or an opinion about the future: I'll help you. It will be fine.

drills.sentence: We're {{answer}} to visit Rome in May.
Look at those clouds! It's going to {{answer}}.
I think she {{answer}} pass the exam.
They're going {{answer}} move house next year.
I'm tired — I {{answer}} go to bed.
drills.hint: short form of the instant future

points.title: Some, any, much, many
points.function: Talk about amounts without counting
points.explanation:
some in positives and offers: some water, some friends. any in negatives and questions: not any milk, any questions?

much with uncountables (much time), many with countables (many people) — mostly in negatives and questions.

points.title: Adverbs of frequency
points.function: Say how often — and put the word in the right place
points.explanation:
always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never.

They sit BEFORE a main verb (I always walk) but AFTER be (she is always late).

drills.sentence: I {{answer}} drink coffee in the morning — every single day.
He is {{answer}} late — it drives me crazy.
We {{answer}} go out on Fridays — maybe once a month.
She {{answer}} eats meat — she's almost vegetarian.
I have {{answer}} been to Japan, but I'd love to go.
They {{answer}} visit us at Christmas — most years.

drills.translation: A regular but not constant habit.
drills.hint: many times — more than sometimes, less than always

points.title: Can, could, have to
points.function: Ability, polite requests, and obligation
points.explanation:
can = ability or permission now: I can swim. could = past ability or a polite request: Could you help me?

have to = obligation from outside: I have to work tomorrow. Negative don't have to = no obligation (different from mustn't!).

drills.sentence: I {{answer}} to wear a uniform at work.
When I was five, I {{answer}} already read.
You don't {{answer}} to come if you're busy.

points.title: Prepositions of time: in, on, at
points.function: Put events on the calendar correctly
points.explanation:
at + clock times and points: at six, at night, at the weekend (BrE).

on + days and dates: on Monday, on 4 July.

in + longer periods: in May, in 2020, in the morning.

points.title: Present perfect vs past simple
points.function: The past that touches now vs the finished past
points.explanation:
Present perfect (have + past participle) links past to now: I've lost my keys (they're still lost). Experience: I've been to Peru. Cues: ever, never, just, already, yet, since, for.

A finished time (yesterday, in 2019, last week) forces the past simple: I lost them yesterday.

drills.sentence: I've {{answer}} my wallet — can you help me look?
We've known each other {{answer}} ten years.

points.title: First and second conditionals
points.function: Real futures and imagined presents
points.explanation:
First (real): If it rains, we'll stay in — if + present, will + verb.

Second (imagined): If I had more time, I would travel — if + past, would + verb. The past form doesn't mean past time; it means 'not real'.

drills.sentence: If it {{answer}} tomorrow, we'll cancel the picnic.
If I {{answer}} rich, I would buy an island.
She {{answer}} travel more if she had time.
If we {{answer}} now, we'll catch the train.
What {{answer}} you do if you won the lottery?

points.title: The passive (is made, was built)
points.function: Focus on what happens, not who does it
points.explanation:
be + past participle: This cheese is made in France. The bridge was built in 1932.

Use it when the doer is unknown, obvious, or unimportant. Name the doer with by only when it matters.

drills.sentence: The castle was {{answer}} in the twelfth century.
These photos {{answer}} taken by my grandmother.
English {{answer}} spoken all over the world.
The thief was caught {{answer}} the police.
My phone was {{answer}} at the festival.

points.title: Relative clauses (who, which, that, where)
points.function: Glue two ideas into one sentence
points.explanation:
who for people, which for things, that for either (defining only), where for places, whose for possession.

The woman who lives next door. The book that changed my life. You can drop the pronoun when it's the object: the film (that) we saw.

drills.sentence: She's the doctor {{answer}} treated my father.
That's the restaurant {{answer}} we first met.
The laptop {{answer}} I bought last week is broken.
He's the author {{answer}} books sell millions.
The train, {{answer}} was already late, then broke down.

points.title: Used to — past habits and states
points.function: Talk about how things were, but aren't anymore
points.explanation:
used to + verb: I used to smoke (I don't now). There used to be a cinema here.

Negative and question drop the d: didn't use to, Did you use to…? Don't confuse with be used to (= be accustomed to).

drills.sentence: I {{answer}} to play football every weekend.
She didn't {{answer}} to like coffee, but now she loves it.
There {{answer}} to be a bakery on this corner.
Did you {{answer}} to live in London?
We {{answer}} to go camping every summer as kids.

points.title: Reported speech
points.function: Pass on what someone said
points.explanation:
Statements step one tense back: 'I'm tired' → she said she was tired. 'I'll come' → he said he would come.

Questions use if/whether or the question word, with normal word order: she asked if I was ready; he asked where I lived.

drills.sentence: 'I'll call you.' → He said he {{answer}} call me.
'Are you ready?' → She asked {{answer}} I was ready.
'Where do you live?' → He asked where I {{answer}}.
'I can swim.' → She said she {{answer}} swim.
'We bought a house.' → They said they {{answer}} bought a house.

points.title: Gerund or infinitive?
points.function: Know which verb pattern follows which verb
points.explanation:
Gerund (-ing) after enjoy, finish, avoid, mind, suggest: I enjoy cooking.

Infinitive (to + verb) after want, decide, hope, plan, promise: I decided to leave.

Some verbs take both with a meaning change: stop smoking (quit) vs stop to smoke (pause in order to).

drills.sentence: I really enjoy {{answer}} in the sea.

points.title: Third and mixed conditionals
points.function: Regret and rewrite the past
points.explanation:
Third (unreal past): If I had known, I would have come — if + past perfect, would have + participle.

Mixed (past cause, present result): If I had saved money, I would be rich now.

drills.sentence: If you {{answer}} told me, I would have helped.
If she had left earlier, she would {{answer}} caught the flight.
I wouldn't {{answer}} met her if I hadn't gone to that party.
If he had studied medicine, he {{answer}} be a doctor now.
If we {{answer}} taken the map, we wouldn't have got lost.
What would you have {{answer}} in my position?

points.title: Modals of deduction (must, might, can't)
points.function: Say how sure you are
points.explanation:
must = almost certain it's true: she must be home, the lights are on. can't = almost certain it's false: that can't be right.

might/may/could = possible. For the past add have: he must have missed the train.

drills.sentence: The lights are on — someone {{answer}} be home.
That {{answer}} be Maria — she's in Brazil this week!
He {{answer}} be stuck in traffic — it happens.
They can't {{answer}} left already — the car's still here.
It {{answer}} rain later, so take an umbrella.

points.title: Advanced passives (has been done, being done)
points.function: Passives across every tense
points.explanation: Perfect: the road has been closed. Continuous: the house is being painted. Modal: the form must be signed. Reporting: he is said to be rich.

drills.sentence: The road has {{answer}} closed since Monday.
Our office is {{answer}} renovated at the moment.
All answers must be {{answer}} in ink.
She is {{answer}} to be the richest woman in the country.
The results will {{answer}} announced on Friday.
drills.hint: the bare infinitive of the passive

drills.sentence: By 1990, the bridge {{answer}} been rebuilt twice.

points.title: Wish and if only
points.function: Regret the present and the past
points.explanation:
wish + past = a present regret: I wish I knew. wish + past perfect = a past regret: I wish I had gone.

wish + would = complaining about others: I wish you would listen.

drills.sentence: I wish I {{answer}} how to drive.
She wishes she {{answer}} accepted the offer.
I wish you {{answer}} stop interrupting me!
If {{answer}} I had listened to your advice!
We wish we {{answer}} bought tickets earlier.

points.title: Phrasal verbs — separable or not?
points.function: Put the object in the right place
points.explanation:
Separable: turn the light off / turn off the light — but a pronoun must split: turn it off (never turn off it).

Inseparable: look after the kids → look after them. You have to learn which is which.

drills.sentence: Could you turn {{answer}} off? The music's too loud.
We've put {{answer}} the meeting until Friday.
He takes {{answer}} his father — same smile, same laugh.

points.title: Question tags and indirect questions
points.function: Soften questions and check what you believe
points.explanation:
Tags flip the polarity: You're coming, aren't you? She doesn't drive, does she?

Indirect questions use statement order: Could you tell me where the station is? (not 'where is the station').

drills.sentence: Could you tell me where the bank {{answer}}?
Do you know if the shop {{answer}} at six?
I wonder {{answer}} they got home safely.

points.title: Inversion (Never have I…, Not only…)
points.function: Flip the word order for emphasis
points.explanation:
After negative openers the auxiliary comes before the subject: Never have I seen such a mess. Not only did he lie, he stole.

Openers: never, rarely, seldom, not only, no sooner … than, under no circumstances, only then.

drills.sentence: Not only {{answer}} she sing, she writes her own songs.
No sooner had we sat down {{answer}} the phone rang.
Under no circumstances {{answer}} you open that door.
Only then {{answer}} I understand what she meant.

points.title: Cleft sentences (What I need is…, It was… that)
points.function: Point the spotlight at one part of the sentence
points.explanation:
What-cleft: What I need is a holiday. It-cleft: It was Anna who broke the news.

The cleft turns one flat sentence into a focus and a background — essential for natural emphasis in writing.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} I need is a good night's sleep.
It was Marco {{answer}} suggested the idea.
What annoys me {{answer}} his constant lateness.
It {{answer}} in Paris that they first met.
{{answer}} she wants is a little respect.
It wasn't until midnight {{answer}} he finally called.

points.title: Hedging and distancing (would seem, tend to)
points.function: Sound measured, academic, and deniable
points.explanation:
English softens claims: it would seem that, this suggests, there appears to be, X tends to.

The passive distances further: it is believed that, it has been argued that. Essential for academic and professional register.

drills.sentence: It would {{answer}} that the figures are wrong.
House prices {{answer}} to rise in spring.
There {{answer}} to be a problem with the data.
It is widely {{answer}} that the climate is changing.
The results {{answer}} that more research is needed.
It could be {{answer}} that the policy failed.

points.title: Participle clauses (Having finished, …)
points.function: Tighten two sentences into one
points.explanation:
Present participle for same-time actions: Walking home, I saw Ana. Perfect participle for earlier actions: Having finished the report, she left.

The implied subject must match the main clause — or you've written a dangling modifier.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} finished dinner, we went for a walk.
{{answer}} through the window, she saw the storm coming.
{{answer}} in 1889, the tower still draws millions.
Not {{answer}} what to say, he stayed silent.
Having {{answer}} in Rome for years, she speaks fluent Italian.
{{answer}} by the news, they cancelled the trip.

points.title: The mandative subjunctive (insist that he be)
points.function: The formal demand pattern
points.explanation:
After demand, insist, recommend, suggest, essential, vital — formal English uses the bare form for all persons: they insisted that he BE present; it is vital that she ARRIVE on time.

British English often uses should instead: insisted that he should be present.

drills.sentence: The board insisted that he {{answer}} removed from the project.
drills.translation: Bare form of be, all persons.

drills.sentence: It is essential that every member {{answer}} the code of conduct.
She recommended that the report {{answer}} rewritten.
It's vital that she {{answer}} informed at once.
We suggest that he {{answer}} a lawyer before signing.

points.title: Ellipsis and substitution (so, neither, do so)
points.function: Leave out what the listener already knows
points.explanation:
so/neither echo: I love jazz — So do I. He can't swim — Neither can I.

do so replaces a whole verb phrase in formal prose: asked to leave, he did so at once. if so / if not compress conditions.

drills.sentence: I loved the film. — {{answer}} did I!
She can't make it tonight. — {{answer}} can I.
He was asked to apologise, and he did {{answer}} immediately.
You may be tired; if {{answer}}, rest.
They promised to help, but failed to {{answer}} so.
I've never been to Oslo. — Nor {{answer}} I.

points.title: Formal connectors (notwithstanding, albeit)
points.function: The registers of written argument
points.explanation:
notwithstanding = despite (can follow its noun: objections notwithstanding). albeit = although + reduced phrase: a success, albeit a costly one.

hence/thus = therefore; whereby = by which; hitherto = until now. Legal and academic prose expects them.

drills.sentence: The plan went ahead, {{answer}} the objections.
It was a victory, {{answer}} a narrow one.
The costs rose sharply; {{answer}} the project was cancelled.
A scheme {{answer}} employees share the profits.
A {{answer}} unknown author won the prize.
The evidence, {{answer}} incomplete, points one way.

points.title: Past simple — regular verbs (-ed)
points.function: Say what happened, with -ed verbs
points.explanation:
Regular verbs form the past with -ed: work → worked, play → played. Spelling: verbs ending in -e just add -d (live → lived); consonant + y becomes -ied (study → studied); short stressed vowels double the final consonant (stop → stopped).

The -ed has three sounds: /t/ after voiceless sounds (worked), /d/ after voiced sounds (lived), and /ɪd/ after t or d (wanted).

Questions and negatives use did + base verb — the -ed disappears: Did you work? I didn't work.

references.title: British Council–EAQUALS Core Inventory for General English

related.title: Past simple — irregular verbs
related.contrast: Irregular verbs change instead of taking -ed: go → went, buy → bought.

related.title: Do-support: questions and negation
related.contrast: did is do-support in the past: Did you work? I didn't work.

drills.sentence: I {{answer}} in a bank for five years.
She {{answer}} in Rome when she was young.
We {{answer}} football in the park yesterday.
They {{answer}} work yesterday — it was a holiday.

points.title: Object pronouns (me, him, them)
points.function: Say who receives the action
points.explanation:
Every subject pronoun has an object partner: I → me, you → you, he → him, she → her, it → it, we → us, they → them. Use the object form after a verb (She knows me) and after a preposition (with him, for us).

English word order does the work a case system would: subject before the verb, object after it.

references.title: British Council–EAQUALS Core Inventory for General English

related.title: Subject pronouns (I, you, he…)
related.contrast: I know him, he knows me — subject before the verb, object after.

drills.sentence: I like these shoes — I'll buy {{answer}}.
Where is my phone? I can't find {{answer}}.

points.title: Imperatives (Open…, Don't…)
points.function: Tell or ask someone to do something
points.explanation:
To tell someone to do something, use the base verb with no subject: Open the window. Come in. Add please to soften it.

The negative uses don't + base verb — for EVERY verb, including be: Don't worry. Don't be late.
points.culture_note: Bare imperatives can sound abrupt in English — natives soften constantly: Could you open the window? rather than Open the window.

references.title: British Council–EAQUALS Core Inventory for General English

related.title: Do-support: questions and negation
related.contrast: The same do appears in the negative imperative: Don't open it.

drills.translation: Asking someone to open the door.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} be late — the film starts at eight.
drills.translation: Warning someone not to be late.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} quiet, please — the baby is sleeping.
Please {{answer}} down and wait a moment.
